explanation: «He gets by. He understands more than he can say» — he takes in more than he produces. The joke about secrets is an inference from that, not a separate fact.
prompt: What lies behind Katya’s remark about keeping secrets from her father?
options: He understands enough Russian to follow the gist
He understands no Russian at all
He banned Russian at home
She and her mother used Russian deliberately in front of him
answer: He understands enough Russian to follow the gist

explanation: Don’t think twice means «don’t hesitate for a second». It comes out as a set phrase, which is why it is delivered so fast.
answer: Don’t think twice. It’s a real gift

explanation: The trap is in Josh’s question — «does your mother not speak English very well?» Katya contradicts it: «she speaks English exceptionally well».
prompt: Why did Katya’s mother speak only Russian to her?
options: She wanted her children to grow up bilingual
Her English was weak
It was the rule at their school
Her father insisted on splitting the languages
answer: She wanted her children to grow up bilingual

explanation: Pick up a language = acquire it without formal study. In connected speech «picked it up» runs together into what sounds like a single word.
answer: I picked it up in a few months

explanation: Future Perfect: by some point in the future this will already have happened. In connected speech «will have» reduces to «will’ve» and is almost impossible to hear as two words.
text: But in a few years’ time, they will have gone out of fashion

explanation: She introduces the example herself: «And some words also change their meaning». The selfie example was about a word appearing; wireless is about a word moving.
prompt: Why does Claire bring up the word wireless?
options: To show that words can shift meaning, not just appear
To argue that technology is damaging the language
To point out that radio has fallen out of use
To show how quickly new words catch on
answer: To show that words can shift meaning, not just appear

explanation: Her conclusion is the last line of the turn: «People are getting more information, but they’re getting worse at expressing themselves». Spell checkers, if anything, are used too readily.
prompt: What exactly does Rosa see as the problem?
options: People get more information but express themselves worse
People read less than they used to
People have stopped using spell checkers
People use too many new words
answer: People get more information but express themselves worse

explanation: A short pivot line that reframes the whole turn: change in language is not news.
answer: And this is nothing new

explanation: «I appreciate you’re still finding your feet» is the polite concession, and it is followed by «OK, but you must understand…». Everything after that but is the actual message.
prompt: What is Sara’s boss really telling her?
options: It is a warning: no result, no permanent contract
She is pleased with how Sara is settling in
She is offering Sara more time to adjust
She is confirming that the contract is signed
answer: It is a warning: no result, no permanent contract

explanation: Sara answers Alex’s joke about a promotion without committing to anything — and here the evasion carries more meaning than any fact would.
text: Something along those lines, yeah

explanation: The wording «You don’t happen to know…, do you?» is about as tentative as a request gets. Sara knows the odds are poor, which is exactly why she asks it that way.
prompt: Why does Sara ask Alex about an author?
options: She is hoping he has the right contacts
She wants to recommend a book to him
She is checking whether he has read «Solar Wind»
She is inviting him to join her for the interview
answer: She is hoping he has the right contacts

explanation: A joke in reply: «those phrases would come in handy for me too». It is the clue that Sara does not really speak Italian.
answer: I think I need a few of them myself

explanation: The force of the example is in the comparison: 3,000 years against 50. And he states the conclusion outright — «nothing lasts forever».
prompt: Why does he bring in the example of Sumerian?
options: To show that even a dominance lasting millennia comes to an end
To argue that English will outlast every other language
To explain where writing originally came from
To compare Sumerian and Latin for complexity
answer: To show that even a dominance lasting millennia comes to an end

explanation: He does not dispute the fact — he disputes the cause: «that’s more as a result of improved communication, and not because of the spread of English». Separating a fact from its explanation is the core skill at this level.
prompt: What is his position on English threatening other languages?
options: Languages are indeed dying out, but English is not the cause
English is the main cause of languages dying out
No languages are actually dying out
English will displace all other languages within 50 years
answer: Languages are indeed dying out, but English is not the cause

explanation: This is the core of the whole argument: the dominance of English is explained by history, not by the design of the language. He adds «and to some extent, accidental» immediately afterwards.
text: and the reasons for its dominance are largely historical

explanation: The closing formula of the talk. It is delivered noticeably slowly — a rhetorical signal that this is the point to take away.
answer: One thing that’s certain is that nothing lasts forever

explanation: The structure gives it away: «Some people did this as a protest … I didn’t have any kind of burning cause … but, for my own reasons, I was still intrigued.» The example exists only so she can define herself against it.
prompt: Why does she mention protest at all?
options: To make clear that her own motive was different
To explain which cause she was supporting
To argue that silent protest is the most effective kind
To criticise people who protest this way
answer: To make clear that her own motive was different

explanation: Give it a go = try it. The whole monologue lands on this one casual phrase, delivered fast and unstressed.
answer: I recommend giving it a go

explanation: Fit in = be accepted by a group. This is the observation the entire experiment grows out of, and she states it almost in passing.
text: Talking is a way of fitting in

explanation: «He was so caught up in what he was saying he wasn’t very aware of me.» She then turns this into her broadest claim — that all of us are a bit like that.
prompt: Why did the landlord not notice that she had said nothing?
options: He was too absorbed in his own talking
She answered him in writing
He had been told about her experiment
She nodded and hummed in the right places
answer: He was too absorbed in his own talking

explanation: Hold yourself back = stop yourself from reacting. Said quickly, «hold myself» compresses to almost two syllables.
answer: but I had to hold myself back

explanation: The whole answer is built on a shift: «A bit scary at first … and then, you feel really calm, completely in control». Tracking that turn is the task — the individual adjectives on their own would fit several options.
prompt: How does she describe the way the jump felt?
options: Frightening at the start, then calm and almost peaceful
Terrifying from beginning to end
Disappointing compared with parachuting
Too fast to feel anything at all
answer: Frightening at the start, then calm and almost peaceful

explanation: A natural step is how she justifies the risk: not a leap, just the next thing along. Notice she says «feels», not «is» — the claim is about her own sense of readiness.
text: yes it feels like a natural step

explanation: She gives her own figure (about 300) and then the benchmarks she has heard (200, or 500) — the point of the comparison is that she clears the lower bar comfortably.
prompt: Why does she feel ready for this?
options: Her parachuting experience is well above the usual minimum
The instructor told her she was ready
The conditions that day were unusually safe
She has base jumped before in a different country
answer: Her parachuting experience is well above the usual minimum

explanation: The repetition of «definitely» is doing the work here: the second one is stressed and carries the commitment.
answer: Oh yes, definitely. I’m definitely going to do it again

explanation: Two idioms carry it: «don’t beat around the bush» and «there’s a lot to be said for being upfront». Emma’s reply — «I can’t just boot him out!» — shows she has heard it as something much harsher than it was.
prompt: What is Alex actually advising her to do?
options: Say it to him plainly instead of dropping hints
Give him a deadline to move out by
Wait until he notices on his own
Ask a friend to talk to him instead
answer: Say it to him plainly instead of dropping hints

explanation: The longest and most indirect way of putting it that the lesson offers — and Emma reaches for it precisely because the request is awkward. Note the gerund after «possibility of».
text: have you thought about the possibility of finding your own place to live

explanation: «There’s nothing else to say about Solar Wind! The book is the book.» His line about not wanting Emma to listen comes later and is a reaction to her teasing, not the cause.
prompt: Why is Max so tense about the interview?
options: He feels he has nothing left to say about his book
He has never spoken on the radio before
He does not want his sister to hear it
He thinks the interviewer will be hostile
answer: He feels he has nothing left to say about his book

explanation: Three unstressed syllables and one stressed one. The phrase plays down the very thing Alex is most excited about.
answer: It’s no big deal

explanation: He separates himself from the complainers twice: «that didn’t surprise me», «I don’t mind paying — it’s just it needs to be something I really want to do». His objection is to the activities, not the bill.
prompt: What is Paolo’s position on the cost?
options: He does not mind paying, as long as it is for something he wants
He thinks the programme should pay for everything
He was surprised at how expensive the activities were
He refused to take part because of the price
answer: He does not mind paying, as long as it is for something he wants

explanation: A superlative plus a present perfect — the standard way of ranking an experience against everything in your life so far. Notice she keeps it in the present: for her the sensation is still live.
text: the initial freefall is the most incredible sensation I’ve ever felt

explanation: He is careful to say the trips themselves were fine — «we had a good enough time just with ourselves». The complaint is about the organisers being absent, and he closes it with a understatement: «Not very friendly, I thought.»
prompt: What does Mehmet find strange about the trips?
options: Only a driver went with them, with nobody from the programme
The group was much smaller than promised
The two activities were on the same day
Nobody explained the safety rules
answer: Only a driver went with them, with nobody from the programme

explanation: A concession in six words. «Worth it» carries the stress, and it is what turns a complaint about price into a recommendation.
answer: but I think it was worth it

explanation: The concession is explicit — «I am in no way trying to criticise the good intentions» — and the sentence that follows reverses it: «But I also now know that good intentions are not enough.» Missing the concession would flip her whole position.
prompt: What is she careful to say about volunteers’ motives?
options: Their intentions are sincere, but good intentions are not enough
Their intentions are usually selfish
Only experienced volunteers have honest motives
Motives matter more than results
answer: Their intentions are sincere, but good intentions are not enough

explanation: Seven words that carry the argument of the entire talk. The stress falls on people, and the auxiliary «do» at the end is what makes the contrast audible.
answer: schools don’t teach kids, people do

explanation: The word now is doing quiet work here: it marks a change of mind, which is what licenses her to criticise a position she used to hold herself.
text: But I also now know that good intentions are not enough

explanation: The structure to catch is «not only … but also»: failing the communities, and setting the travellers, and society with them, up for failure. Hearing only the first half gives you the wrong answer.
prompt: According to her, who is harmed by volunteer travel?
options: Both the communities and the volunteers themselves
The communities only
The volunteers only
Neither — the harm is exaggerated
answer: Both the communities and the volunteers themselves

explanation: The understatement is the point: no adjectives, no anger, just what was there. Half-empty is stressed on the first element.
answer: I found a half-empty building

explanation: «Made a terrible mess of it, ended up sideways, hit this big rock in the middle of the river.» He is describing bad technique rather than disaster — which is why the consequence is only water in the boat.
prompt: What went wrong in the rapids?
options: He was turned sideways and hit a rock in mid-river
His kayak overturned completely
He lost his paddle in the current
He was swept past his planned stopping point
answer: He was turned sideways and hit a rock in mid-river

explanation: Six words that set the atmosphere of the whole first entry — and they follow a complaint about the boat scraping, so the contrast marker «but» carries real weight.
text: but it’s so quiet out here

explanation: He says it as an aside — «my hammock’s on the floor because … I’m in a little stone island» — and never finishes the reason. The listener has to complete the logic themselves.
prompt: Why is his hammock on the floor?
options: He is camped on a bare stone island with nothing to hang it from
The hammock is damaged
He wants to stay closer to the boat
It is too windy to hang it up
answer: He is camped on a bare stone island with nothing to hang it from

explanation: Catch your breath = recover after effort. He is speaking while still breathing hard, so the phrase runs together almost into one word.
answer: I’m just trying to catch my breath

explanation: «It was an outright disaster! I came across as a blithering idiot!» Note come across as — it is about the impression he made, not about the facts of what he said.
prompt: How does Max describe his own performance?
options: A complete disaster in which he sounded like an idiot
Uncomfortable at first but acceptable overall
Fine, except for one bad question
Better than he had expected
answer: A complete disaster in which he sounded like an idiot

explanation: Sum up = state the essential point briefly. The hedge «just about» is what makes the line funny: he has just delivered anything but a brief summary.
text: Yes, I think that just about sums it up

explanation: «When the light turns red, we’re live.» It is said once, in passing, as studio routine — and it is the only signal Max is given.
prompt: How will Max know that the interview has started?
options: A light will turn red
Oscar will introduce him by name
Headphones will be handed to him
A producer will count him in
answer: A light will turn red

explanation: A three-word discourse marker that introduces a summary. It is unstressed and runs straight into the next clause.
answer: All things considered

explanation: «I suppose that’s true in a way … But if you can look beyond that … it’s a magical place.» The concession is real, and so is the reversal — the whole meaning sits in the word but.
prompt: What is Annie’s attitude to the effect of tourism?
options: She concedes the point but says you can look past it
She denies that tourism has changed the city
She agrees that the city is no longer worth visiting
She thinks tourism has improved the old centre
answer: She concedes the point but says you can look past it

explanation: He uses his friends as evidence rather than as opinion — a detail a travel review would keep, because it says more than «it’s crowded» ever could.
prompt: What does Roberto report about people who live there?
options: They now go to less touristy parts of the city
They are leaving Cusco altogether
They welcome the extra visitors
They rarely mention tourism at all
answer: They now go to less touristy parts of the city

explanation: Two hedges in one short sentence — «I think» and «a bit» — soften a fairly harsh judgement. Remove them and Roberto sounds like a different, angrier person.
text: I think it’s been a bit spoiled by tourism

explanation: A bowl in the mountains — a single image doing the work of a paragraph. Note the hedge «a kind of», which marks it as his own metaphor rather than a geographical term.
prompt: How does Roberto describe the setting of the city?
options: It lies in a kind of bowl in the mountains
It is spread along a river valley
It stands on a high plateau
It is built around a lake
answer: It lies in a kind of bowl in the mountains

explanation: Four words that decide Annie’s whole review. They come straight after a concession, which is what gives them their force.
answer: it’s a magical place

explanation: «Not everybody admits or acknowledges that they sometimes act upon it — it’s seen as something mysterious.» The hesitation is professional and cultural, not a rule.
prompt: Why are doctors reluctant to admit to acting on gut feeling?
options: It is seen as something mysterious rather than proper medicine
They are forbidden to record it in patient notes
Patients complain when they hear about it
It has repeatedly been shown to be wrong
answer: It is seen as something mysterious rather than proper medicine

explanation: The single most quotable line of the discussion. Predictor carries the main stress and is the word a summary would have to keep.
answer: gut feeling is the most powerful predictor

explanation: This is the sentence that reframes the whole debate: instinct is not a mystical extra, it is what fills the gap left by missing tests. Deal with is the verb that carries the argument.
text: In general practice we have to deal with a lot of uncertainty

explanation: The hedge «kind of» sits inside the phrase and swallows most of its own vowels — typical of unscripted expert speech.
answer: that kind of goes under-explored

explanation: The chain has four links — bag search, security guard, his policeman friend, the earlier report of a strange theft — and it is told at speed. Any one link on its own would not answer the question.
prompt: How were the burglars caught?
options: A stolen toy was found in a bag searched at a football ground
A neighbour recognised one of them in the street
They tried to sell the toys back to the family
The police found fingerprints in the kitchen
answer: A stolen toy was found in a bag searched at a football ground

explanation: A dead idiom brought back to life by the story around it. «Literally» is doing real work here for once — she was described as a statue of ice a minute earlier.
text: and that literally broke the ice

explanation: She is careful about her own memory: «at first, I didn’t completely understand what was going on, but I think I picked up on the general anxiety». The hedges mark this as reconstruction, not recall.
prompt: Why did Marissa cry that evening?
options: She picked up on the anxiety around her
She had been told her brother was in danger
She was frightened of the forest
She had been searching for hours herself
answer: She picked up on the anxiety around her

explanation: Four flat words, repeated twice in his account. The stress lands on not, and that is what makes it a correction rather than a comment.
answer: That’s just not true

explanation: The phrase sounds deferential and does the opposite: «I don’t think you’re in a position to tell us what does and doesn’t make a good interviewer.» This is the sharpest thing said in the whole meeting.
prompt: What is Nadia telling Sara with «With all due respect»?
options: That Sara is not senior enough to judge interviewing
That Sara has misunderstood the facts
That she agrees with Sara but cannot say so
That Sara should apologise to Oscar
answer: That Sara is not senior enough to judge interviewing

explanation: Hit the nail on the head = say exactly the right thing. Seven words, all unstressed except nail and head, which is why fixed idioms are so hard to catch at speed.
text: You’ve hit the nail on the head

explanation: «I’ve read his book and it’s fascinating. He must have loads to say about it.» The modal is a deduction, not a fact — and it makes the failed interview more frustrating, not less.
prompt: Why does Sara think it is a shame about the interview?
options: She has read the book and believes he has a lot to say
She had hoped to conduct the interview herself
She thinks Oscar was treated unfairly
She had recommended Max to the station
answer: She has read the book and believes he has a lot to say

explanation: «We’re the professionals… It’s our job to get the best from our interviewees.» She reframes a complaint about the guest as a question about the team’s competence.
prompt: What does Nadia say the team is forgetting?
options: That getting the best from an interviewee is their job
That the schedule was too tight
That Max had never been interviewed before
That the audience enjoyed it anyway
answer: That getting the best from an interviewee is their job

explanation: Three words that end the argument. Point taken concedes without apologising, which is precisely why it is useful.
answer: OK, point taken

explanation: Both halves are stated: «a real eye-opener in lots of ways» and «I never really felt at home in London». The interviewer supplies the word she is reaching for — «Like an outsider?» — and she accepts it.
prompt: How did she feel after the move to London?
options: Impressed by the variety, but never at home there
Immediately at home in the city
Eager to perform in front of new audiences
Determined to return to South Africa at once
answer: Impressed by the variety, but never at home there

explanation: Roots = where you and your family come from. She answers a question about change with an image of return — which is the whole argument of her story in six words.
text: Oh yeah. Like coming back to my roots

explanation: «I’m really happy about it, yeah, sure. But — it’s not the main thing for me.» She accepts and sidesteps in the same breath; hearing only the first clause would give the wrong answer.
prompt: How does she react to being called a big name?
options: She is pleased but says it is not what matters to her
She rejects the description as inaccurate
She says she has worked hard to earn it
She avoids answering the question
answer: She is pleased but says it is not what matters to her

explanation: Five words that undercut the whole premise of the question. Actually marks the correction, and it is the stressed word in the line.
answer: I was actually really shy

explanation: The reasoning is left to the listener: «Someone French wearing an England shirt? Come on!» Nobody states that the card was checked because of the shirt — you have to join the two facts yourself.
prompt: Why did the immigration officer become suspicious?
options: A French ID card did not fit an England football shirt
The man could not speak French
His passport photograph did not match his face
He had no ticket for the match
answer: A French ID card did not fit an England football shirt

explanation: The co-presenter’s pay-off line. Bumbling = clumsy and incompetent, and it lands on the first syllable.
answer: What you might call a bumbling crook

explanation: A cleft sentence — «what they didn’t realise was that…» — used to hold the surprise back until the very end of the clause. It is the standard way English news builds a punchline.
text: what they didn’t realise was that an emergency services operator could overhear the whole thing

explanation: The smuggled phone is only the tool; the trick itself is the forged domain that made the email look like it came from the authorities. Confusing means with method is the trap here.
prompt: How did Parsons get out of the high-security facility?
options: He emailed officials a release form from a fake domain
He climbed out during building work
He swapped places with a visitor
He bribed a guard with a smuggled phone
answer: He emailed officials a release form from a fake domain

explanation: Nobody’s fool = a person who cannot easily be tricked. Note the contrast marker «even», which concedes that this is an odd thing to say about a convicted fraudster.
answer: But even lawyers and judges agree, John Parsons is nobody’s fool

explanation: «Instead of them paying us, we have to end up paying them for this privilege.» The sarcasm in that last phrase is where her attitude lives.
prompt: What is Olivia’s objection to internships?
options: The intern ends up paying the company rather than being paid
They last far too long
They rarely lead to a permanent job
They are only offered to graduates
answer: The intern ends up paying the company rather than being paid

explanation: Manage to marks an achievement against resistance. Andrew uses it twice in his short turn, and it is the clearest signal of how differently he experienced the job market.
text: I managed to negotiate a higher salary than they were offering

explanation: «There’s too many people these days who are willing to work on a voluntary basis.» He has the degree — the problem is the supply of free labour, and he is part of it himself.
prompt: Why can Mike not find paid conservation work?
options: Too many people are willing to do it for nothing
He lacks the right degree
The reserves are too far from where he lives
He has no practical experience at all
answer: Too many people are willing to do it for nothing

explanation: The line the whole monologue has been building towards. Hardly is a negative adverb, so nothing else in the sentence has to be negative for the meaning to be.
answer: and that’s hardly enough to live on

explanation: If my memory serves me correctly is a formal hedge — and Sara uses it about something she has certainly been told rather than half-remembered. The politeness is doing the work of hiding her research.
text: You’re staying with Emma at the moment, if my memory serves me correctly

explanation: The sequence is what tells you: she asks whether he would still have agreed to meet, he answers «not after that interview with Oscar», she raises another interview — and only then does he stand up.
prompt: Why does Max say it is time to go?
options: He has realised she came for an interview
He is late for another appointment
He is offended by something she said about his book
He has spotted a photographer
answer: He has realised she came for an interview

explanation: Might consider is about as non-committal as English gets — two layers of hedging on a single decision.
answer: Look, I might consider doing another interview

explanation: She does not argue about privacy at all — she reframes the account as part of the job, «particularly of marketing staff». That reframing, not the demand itself, is what ends the interview.
prompt: How does the interviewer justify the demand?
options: Staff must project a positive image at all times, in person and online
It is a legal requirement in the industry
Previous employees have leaked confidential material
Clients ask to see the team’s profiles
answer: Staff must project a positive image at all times, in person and online

explanation: Laila’s whole position compressed into one conditional. It sounds like common sense, and it quietly shifts the responsibility from the employer to the applicant.
text: so if you don’t want people to read it, then don’t post it

explanation: «No more carefully than I would if I walked into a room full of people I don’t know. It’s the same thing — what’s the difference?» The rhetorical question is her strongest move, and she leaves it unanswered.
prompt: What comparison does Laila make at the end?
options: Presenting yourself online is like walking into a room of strangers
Social media is like a private diary
Employers are like distant relatives
A profile is like a second CV
answer: Presenting yourself online is like walking into a room of strangers

explanation: Her reaction is a reaction to other people’s reaction: «surprised, because I thought, what do people expect in this day and age?» Following that second layer is what the question tests.
prompt: What surprised Laila about the article she read?
options: That people were surprised by the request at all
That so many employers now ask
That the practice is legal
That applicants usually refuse
answer: That people were surprised by the request at all

explanation: A reference used as an adjective. The hedge «just a bit too» keeps the accusation civil while leaving nobody in any doubt about it.
answer: that’s just a bit too Big Brother

explanation: «I wouldn’t go that far… I’m definitely amateur but I’m not a photographer yet, perhaps in the future.» She rejects the interviewer’s own word — the correction is the answer.
prompt: How does Monika describe herself?
options: An amateur, but not yet a photographer
A professional photographer
A photography teacher
Someone who has given up photography
answer: An amateur, but not yet a photographer

explanation: «There was this chair right in front of the hole and it made the picture quite realistic, like there was still life in it — but actually the building is being demolished.» The point is the contradiction, not the subject.
prompt: What made the photograph of the building work, in her view?
options: A chair left in front of the hole, making it look still lived in
The unusual angle of the light
The workers who appear in the frame
The colour of the demolished walls
answer: A chair left in front of the hole, making it look still lived in

explanation: Present perfect continuous for something started in the past and still going on. She uses the same structure three times in the interview — it is the tense of a course in progress.
text: I’ve been doing this course about photography and it’s for complete beginners

explanation: «I could blame my husband for it!» — a joke that carries the actual reason: a complicated DSLR that is useless if you do not know how to work it.
prompt: Why did she sign up for a course?
options: She had been given a camera she could not use properly
She wanted to change career
A friend was doing the same course
Her old camera had broken
answer: She had been given a camera she could not use properly

explanation: Blame someone for something. The whole phrase is delivered as a joke, so it comes out fast and with the stress on «husband».
answer: blame my husband for it

explanation: Touch and go = uncertain, could go either way. The sentence that follows confirms it: «it could have been fatal without urgent medical attention.»
prompt: What does the reporter mean by «it was touch and go»?
options: His life was genuinely in danger at that point
The doctors could not agree on a diagnosis
He recovered almost immediately
The hospital was too busy to admit him
answer: His life was genuinely in danger at that point

explanation: Whoever as a subject — the perfect word for a story where the person cannot be named. It carries the entire mystery of the report in one syllable.
text: Whoever drove Mr Leota got him here just in time

explanation: The reporter hedges it — «evidently, they were in a hurry» — because even this is second-hand. Nobody has spoken to them.
prompt: Why did the couple leave the hospital so quickly?
options: They were both due at important meetings
They did not want any publicity
They were asked to move their car
They had another emergency to attend to
answer: They were both due at important meetings

explanation: Make a full recovery is a fixed medical collocation. Five words, four of them unstressed.
answer: He’ll make a full recovery

explanation: Cross that bridge when you come to it = deal with a problem only if it actually arises. It is her answer to a hostile question, and it works because it refuses the premise without denying it.
text: I’ll just have to cross that bridge when I come to it

explanation: «I think these facts speak for themselves» and then straight into the numbers. Refusing to defend a claim and simply producing evidence is the strongest move available in a meeting.
prompt: How does Sara answer Oscar’s accusation that she is overdoing it?
options: She lists sales and translation figures instead of arguing
She apologises and moves on
She points out that his interview failed
She asks Nadia to intervene
answer: She lists sales and translation figures instead of arguing

explanation: The way to go = the right approach. Delivered warmly and immediately before a refusal to decide, which is what makes it worth noticing.
answer: That’s absolutely the way to go

explanation: «They’ll come up with things like coconuts or avocados, which take years to grow!» The reason is practical and it is given as a joke, which makes it easy to hear as an aside rather than an answer.
prompt: Why do the children not get a completely free choice?
options: They suggest things that would take years to grow
The soil is unsuitable for most plants
The budget only covers certain seeds
The council restricts what can be planted
answer: They suggest things that would take years to grow

explanation: A one-off = something that happens only once. The contrast with «long-term» is the sentence a report would quote, because it answers the obvious objection to volunteering projects.
text: So it’s not just a one-off thing, it has a long-term benefit too

explanation: «Sometimes yes, but we also have a centre, or a few centres round the city, I should say.» The self-correction is what turns one centre into several.
prompt: How does the food reach people?
options: Delivered to homes and also collected from centres
Only delivered directly to homes
Only collected from a single centre
Sold cheaply at a weekly market
answer: Delivered to homes and also collected from centres

explanation: Four words carrying the whole argument for the project. Such a before an uncountable noun is emphatic, and the stress falls on «waste».
answer: It’s such a waste

explanation: The parallel structure — «what was… and what was…» — is what makes the sentence quotable. Note the reduced «we would» to «we’d», which is where the conditional almost disappears.
text: That way we’d know what was fact and what was fiction

explanation: The two signals are given as sound effects — «our phones went Brrrrrr, or the lines on our computer screen popped up in red». Concrete detail delivered fast and easily missed.
prompt: How would the fact-checker let people know?
options: Phones would buzz and screen text would turn red
A message would be sent to the speaker
The claim would be deleted automatically
A report would go to a fact-checking service
answer: Phones would buzz and screen text would turn red

explanation: Seven words admitting that the argument has not been settled. Rare and worth noticing: most speakers in this position would pick a side.
answer: So I’m not sure what I think

explanation: The force of it is in what follows: «There’s nothing stopping me from reading an English-language newspaper in China — it’s just a click away. But I don’t.» The obstacle is habit, not access.
prompt: What example does Zelda use to explain the problem?
options: She reads plenty of news online, but only British papers
She has stopped reading the news altogether
She only follows people she has met in person
She reads translations rather than originals
answer: She reads plenty of news online, but only British papers

explanation: The thesis of the whole programme in one sentence, and it is built on a concession: the tool is not the problem, we are. Exploit to its full potential is a fixed collocation.
text: the Internet is a very powerful tool but very few of us exploit it to its full potential

explanation: The two verbs are the book’s title and its argument. Zelda quotes them and then admits the difficulty at once: «That’s the problem. It’s a challenge.»
prompt: What does Zuckerman say we need to do?
options: Disconnect from our current thinking and rewire
Spend less time online overall
Learn at least one foreign language
Read only international news sources
answer: Disconnect from our current thinking and rewire

explanation: Tend to softens a sweeping claim about everybody. The line is the hinge of the interview and is delivered quite slowly.
answer: We tend to think we know more than we actually do

explanation: He states two grievances and the second is the live one: «First, you hide the fact that you’re a journalist. Then, you go gossiping about me to the whole world!» The idea itself is never criticised.
prompt: What exactly has annoyed Max?
options: That she discussed his work with Alex behind his back
That the prequel idea is a bad one
That she interviewed him without asking
That his publisher phoned during their meeting
answer: That she discussed his work with Alex behind his back

explanation: «I’m scared that I’ll ruin Solar Wind by writing something rubbish that everyone hates.» Everything before this — the anger at Sara, the anger at Emma — has been a way of not saying it.
prompt: What does Max finally admit?
options: He is afraid a second book would ruin the first
He has already started writing a prequel
He does not want to be a writer any more
He has run out of money
answer: He is afraid a second book would ruin the first

explanation: He says so plainly — and Sara’s reply, «most writers would be over the moon», is what makes his reaction so revealing.
prompt: What was the phone call about?
options: His publisher asking about another book
A newspaper requesting an interview
His sister asking when he would be home
A reader complaining about the ending
answer: His publisher asking about another book

explanation: Three words, said quietly, that turn the whole scene around. Everything loud in this episode has been protecting this sentence.
answer: I’m scared, Em

explanation: And she names the cause herself: «this kind of precision affects the way he works». The morning biscuit is not a digression — it is the evidence for the diagnosis.
prompt: What frustrates Masha about Sam?
options: He will not start until he understands everything
He never finishes what he starts
He changes his mind constantly
He refuses to attend meetings
answer: He will not start until he understands everything

explanation: A cleft sentence — «what you need … is …» — used to give a criticism the weight of a general truth. Claudio is talking about Masha without naming her once.
text: what you need more than anything else is the ability to laugh at yourself

explanation: «The main reason why this is happening is that they just can’t seem to communicate well with each other.» Everything else she says is evidence for that single claim.
prompt: What is Vicki’s diagnosis of the team?
options: They cannot communicate well with each other
They lack the right technical skills
They are given too much work
They do not respect her authority
answer: They cannot communicate well with each other

explanation: Pull together = work cooperatively. Six words summarising a whole management problem, and the phrasal verb carries all of it.
answer: We’re not pulling together as a team

explanation: «Most of my work comes from dreams. Quite literally I’m a surrealist, I suppose.» The night waking is not an interruption to his job — it is where the job starts.
prompt: What does Bernie do with what he draws at night?
options: It becomes the basis of his artistic work
He shows it to his students the next day
He throws it away in the morning
He uses it to remember his dreams only
answer: It becomes the basis of his artistic work

explanation: A cleft opening — «one thing we know for sure is…» — used to separate the certain from the speculative. The emphatic «do» is what makes the claim sound settled.
text: One thing we know for sure is that we do need to sleep

explanation: The presenter offers eight hours and the researcher rejects it — «Not necessarily». The answer is in the correction, not in the question.
prompt: What does the research say about the ideal length of sleep?
options: Between six-and-a-half and seven-and-a-half hours
Exactly eight hours
At least nine hours
It varies too much to measure
answer: Between six-and-a-half and seven-and-a-half hours

explanation: The turning point in Matt’s account. Actually marks the surprise — most people would describe insomnia as time lost.
answer: Well, I actually decided to use the time creatively

explanation: Twelve words that describe the entire regime, delivered as a flat answer to a yes/no question. Note the pair «weigh and measure» — two verbs where one would do, which is itself the point.
text: I weigh and measure everything I eat and almost never eat out

explanation: Two strategies given as alternatives — «either take some nuts, which I weigh beforehand… or I drink some water or tea round there». Both have to be caught for the answer to be complete.
prompt: How does he manage social occasions in restaurants?
options: He brings pre-weighed nuts, or just drinks something
He orders the plainest thing on the menu
He avoids social occasions altogether
He eats beforehand and explains why
answer: He brings pre-weighed nuts, or just drinks something

explanation: Every positive comment he makes is framed as a negative that failed to materialise. That is a verdict on his own expectations as much as on the food.
prompt: What is the reporter’s overall verdict on the taste?
options: Better than he had expected, though not to his taste
Genuinely delicious
Completely inedible
Impossible to judge from one spoonful
answer: Better than he had expected, though not to his taste

explanation: Six words, and the meaning sits entirely in «actually» — the surprise is that his low expectations were not met.
answer: It’s actually not that bad

explanation: The most polite possible framing of the least honourable request in the scene. «Would you mind + -ing» keeps it casual; the journalist’s reply — «Freelance and anonymous, eh?» — does not.
text: Would you mind keeping my name off the article

explanation: And the flexibility comes with a threat attached: «if I don’t get it by two and it’s not the kind of thing I’m looking for, the deal’s off.» Two conditions, not one.
prompt: What happens to the deadline during the call?
options: She moves it from one o’clock to two
She removes it entirely
She brings it forward to midday
She agrees to publish the next day
answer: She moves it from one o’clock to two

explanation: The most ordinary line in the episode and the most consequential. It is delivered fast and casually, which is exactly why nobody in the room questions it.
answer: Just forgot my car keys again

explanation: The reasoning runs backwards through the interview: bodies are still adapted to the Stone Age, so «that explains why many people find these foods difficult to digest». The conclusion is stated before the evidence.
prompt: Why, in her view, do many people struggle with dairy and grains?
options: Our bodies never adapted to foods that arrived late
Modern processing damages them
They are eaten in the wrong combinations
They are usually eaten too quickly
answer: Our bodies never adapted to foods that arrived late

explanation: The whole premise of the restaurant in one contrast. Note that she says «the same people», not «the same bodies» — a slightly bigger claim than the evidence supports.
text: We’ve got different technology but we’re the same people as we were then

explanation: The ingredients are ancient, the cooking is not — «to use these ingredients, but, of course, to create sophisticated, modern and very tasty dishes».
prompt: How does she describe the cooking at Ancestors?
options: Sophisticated modern dishes made from those ingredients
Meat and nuts served as they would have been then
Traditional recipes from several ancient cultures
Raw food with no cooking at all
answer: Sophisticated modern dishes made from those ingredients

explanation: Her final claim, and the one with no evidence behind it. Six words, all short, running together at the end of a persuasive turn.
answer: they’re eating what’s good for them

explanation: The joke lands on the contradiction and she leaves it there: «that’s a relief — but stress level high, it says. Ah well… what can you do?»
prompt: What does the health screen tell Rita?
options: Everything is normal except that her stress level is high
Everything is completely normal
She should see a doctor immediately
The reading has failed
answer: Everything is normal except that her stress level is high

explanation: Let someone know = inform them, less formal than «tell» or «report to». The whole pitch of the app is in this one relaxed phrase: the city becomes something you message.
text: People use their mobiles to let the city know where there’s a problem

explanation: She corrects the expectation before describing it: «this isn’t an app, but it’s an information screen». The correction is the most important thing in the sentence.
prompt: What is Rita describing?
options: A public screen that checks your health
A phone app for booking appointments
A fitness tracker worn on the wrist
A pharmacy service for elderly people
answer: A public screen that checks your health

explanation: Five words with «of course» doing the work: he is presenting free charging as the only imaginable arrangement.
answer: It’s all free, of course

explanation: Katie gives two objections and only the second one settles it. «A bit old-fashioned» is taste; «we won’t get a table» is a fact, and facts end arguments faster.
prompt: Why is the Raffles Hotel ruled out?
options: You have to book well in advance and they will not get a table
It is too expensive for their budget
It is closed for renovation
It is too far from everything else
answer: You have to book well in advance and they will not get a table

explanation: Seven words that change the register of the conversation. Until this point they are each arguing for a preference; after it they are solving a scheduling problem together.
text: we’ve only got a day, let’s compromise

explanation: She gives the consequence first — «you’ll wake up in the middle of the night» — and the reason after. Advice in English very often arrives in that order.
prompt: Why does Katie want to stay awake?
options: Moving about helps with jetlag
They have somewhere to be that evening
She is not tired at all
She wants to see the city at night
answer: Moving about helps with jetlag

explanation: Skip = leave out. Katie gives up her own suggestion here, which is the moment the plan finally becomes possible.
answer: Let’s skip the museum and visit the SkyPark

explanation: «Absolutely not. And I’d appreciate it if you didn’t try to contact me again. It’s over. Finished. Finito. The end.» The four-way repetition is the measure of how final he means it.
prompt: How does Max end the phone call?
options: He refuses to meet and asks not to be contacted again
He agrees to come in and discuss it
He threatens legal action
He accepts her apology
answer: He refuses to meet and asks not to be contacted again

explanation: Fall into place = start working out well. It is the last line of the episode before everything stops working out, which is presumably the point.
text: It seems like everything’s falling into place at last

explanation: It is an argument from motive, not from character — «why would Sara give the exclusive information that she needs…?» That is what makes it convincing.
prompt: Why does Emma doubt that Sara leaked the story?
options: Sara needs that information for her own big break
Sara does not know the title of the book
Sara was not in the office that night
Sara has no contacts in the press
answer: Sara needs that information for her own big break

explanation: Four words in the present continuous used for something that has not been agreed. The grammar itself is the threat.
answer: You’re going to resign

explanation: It takes almost a minute to get there because Ron notices her face before he hears her news: «you don’t exactly look like you’re jumping for joy».
prompt: What are they actually celebrating?
options: Lizzie’s son has won a university scholarship
Lizzie has been promoted
Ron’s daughter has finished her degree
It is Lizzie’s birthday
answer: Lizzie’s son has won a university scholarship

explanation: Leave the nest = grow up and move out of the family home. Ron reaches for the idiom rather than say anything direct, which is what makes the sympathy bearable.
text: it’s hard when they leave the nest

explanation: «It just hit me, y’know, this whole thing of Josh leaving home.» The pride is real and so is the loss — the task is to hold both at once.
prompt: Why is Lizzie not simply delighted?
options: Because her son will be leaving home
Because the scholarship is not enough money
Because she wanted him to study elsewhere
Because she does not think he will cope
answer: Because her son will be leaving home

explanation: Three words rejecting somebody else’s description of your own feelings. Short, precise, and the most important line she says.
answer: Thoughtful — not miserable

explanation: The speech itself goes well — «got a few laughs at my jokes». The damage is invisible to him until afterwards, which is why «Dan was giving me these funny looks» is the key line.
prompt: What exactly went wrong with Rob’s speech?
options: One card was missing, and it was the one thanking the bride’s parents
He lost all his cards on the way to the church
His jokes fell flat with the guests
He spoke for far too long
answer: One card was missing, and it was the one thanking the bride’s parents

explanation: If only + past perfect for a regret about something unchangeable. Six words, and the contraction «I’d» is the whole grammar point.
text: If only I’d checked those cards

explanation: Get a grip on yourself = regain self-control. He is quoting his own thoughts, so it comes out fast and half-swallowed.
answer: Come on, get a grip on yourself

explanation: The presenter summarises it and she refines the summary: «or at least it gives them the feeling that they’re more in control». That correction is the most precise thing in the interview.
prompt: Why do rituals appeal to athletes, according to Sandy?
options: They give back a sense of control during a helpless wait
They help pass the time before an event
They are required by most coaches
They distract from the pain of training
answer: They give back a sense of control during a helpless wait

explanation: A quotation inside an interview, built as a chain: comfortable → confident → win. The repetition of «comfortable» is what makes the chain audible.
text: These rituals make me feel comfortable and when I feel comfortable I get confident

explanation: Both speakers use the same word — «not directly, anyway» — and the whole distinction between magic and psychology lives in that adverb.
prompt: Can a ritual affect the outcome of a game?
options: Not directly, but possibly through confidence
Yes, in ways science cannot yet explain
No, and the belief makes things worse
Only for very experienced athletes
answer: Not directly, but possibly through confidence

explanation: The conclusion of the whole interview in eight words. Believed carries the stress, and the causal link is stated with no hedging at all.
answer: Because they believed in it, they performed better

explanation: The correction is precise and deliberate: «Sara wasn’t idly gossiping. She was updating me on her research and was overheard by another journalist.» The distinction between gossip and being overheard is the whole point of the call.
prompt: What has Nadia found out?
options: Sara was overheard by another journalist
Sara sold the story herself
The article was written by a stranger
The leak came from Max’s publisher
answer: Sara was overheard by another journalist

explanation: «Before we get started, can I just make a point about creativity?» He is correcting the premise of the question he has not yet been asked — and Sara’s reply, «I’m glad to hear that», tells you she understands why.
prompt: Why does Max interrupt before answering the first question?
options: To say that creativity does not come easily all the time
To check that the recording has started
To ask for a different question
To thank the station for the second chance
answer: To say that creativity does not come easily all the time

explanation: In a bad place = going through a difficult time emotionally. Nine words that undo everything he was defending in the previous three units.
text: I was in a bad place up until recently

explanation: «At least consider» is doing all the work — she asks for the smallest possible commitment, which is why she gets it.
prompt: What does Nadia ask Max to do?
options: At least consider doing the interview with Sara tomorrow
Issue a public statement about the leak
Meet her at the station to discuss the article
Accept an apology on Oscar’s behalf
answer: At least consider doing the interview with Sara tomorrow

explanation: Seven words spoken slowly and deliberately, in a recording made to be broadcast. That is what makes them an admission rather than a remark.
answer: it doesn’t come easily all the time

explanation: She draws the line by stakes, not by principle: «there’s a difference between the products. But, not for films.» Missing the exception would make her position sound simpler than it is.
prompt: When does Kim read reviews?
options: Before buying expensive things like a laptop or TV
Never, under any circumstances
Only for films she plans to see at the cinema
When friends recommend something
answer: Before buying expensive things like a laptop or TV

explanation: Never dream of doing something = would never even consider it. Fifteen words at speed, and the whole sentence hangs on a contraction and a gerund.
text: I’d never dream of going to see a film without checking the reviews online first

explanation: «It’s interesting to compare the review with what I initially thought of the film.» The timing is the whole method — reading first would replace her reaction instead of testing it.
prompt: Why does Marie read reviews after seeing a film?
options: To compare her own reaction with the reviewer’s
To decide whether to see it again
Because she has no time beforehand
To find out what she should have thought
answer: To compare her own reaction with the reviewer’s

explanation: The last words of the last recording of the course. Four words, and definitely takes the stress — the rule matters more to him than the reason for it.
answer: but definitely not before
